target.title: feat(hooks): add agent and tool lifecycle boundaries
target.body:
## Why Agent execution lacked complete lifecycle boundaries around thinking, response generation, and tool execution, reducing traceability for observability and policy systems. This PR adds those boundaries and ensures tool lifecycle hooks carry `toolCallId` context when available. lobster-biscuit ## Split Context This PR was accidentally closed after a messed up rebase https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/12583 which was originally split from closed umbrella PR #9761: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/9761. ## Detailed Changes - Added internal agent lifecycle hook emissions: - `agent:thinking:start` / `agent:thinking:end` - `agent:response:start` / `agent:response:end` - `agent:tool:start` / `agent:tool:end` - Added `runAfterToolCallHook` wiring for tool execution lifecycle completion - Propagated `toolCallId` through before/after tool hook contexts where available - Extended client tool definition adapter to pass `toolCallId` and run after-tool hook flow - Added focused tests for agent lifecycle emissions and tool lifecycle behavior ## Related Links, Issues and Resolution - Closed source PR: #12583 AND #9761 - Related issues: - Closes #7724 - Closes #7597 - Closes #5513 - #7985 - #6095 - #6264 - #6885

Greptile Summary

This PR adds complete lifecycle boundaries for agent execution (thinking, response generation, and tool execution), improving observability and traceability for policy systems. The implementation propagates `toolCallId` through tool lifecycle hooks and ensures consistent hook firing across different execution paths. Key improvements: - Split lifecycle hook logic into dedicated `lifecycle-hooks.ts` module - Ensured `agent:thinking:start/end` fire for all agent runs - Added conditional `agent:response:start/end` hooks that only fire when response output is generated - Propagated `toolCallId` to `before_tool_call` and `after_tool_call` hooks - Added `runAfterToolCallHook` wiring for both success and error paths in tool execution - Comprehensive test coverage validates hook emissions in different scenarios

Confidence Score: 5/5

- Safe to merge - well-structured refactoring with comprehensive test coverage - The changes are well-architected with proper error handling throughout. The new `lifecycle-hooks.ts` module cleanly separates concerns, all hook emission paths include error handling that logs warnings without failing execution, and the test coverage validates the core lifecycle hook behavior. The `toolCallId` propagation is handled consistently with proper fallbacks. - No files require special attention Last reviewed commit: d48824c

target.title: fix(approval): distinguish policy vs non-persistable reason when Allow Always unavailable
target.body:
Fixes #97069.

## What Problem This Solves

When a command includes shell redirection (e.g. `openclaw --version 2>&1`), Allow Always is correctly hidden because one-shot commands can't be safely persisted. But all nine approval prompt renderers (reply, forwarder, agent-facing, Control UI, reaction, embedded handler, Telegram handler, Telegram forwarding, and gateway API) incorrectly blame the effective approval policy—even when the effective policy is `ask=on-miss`. Users waste time misdiagnosing their approval policy.

## Why This Change Was Made

The root cause is that `resolveExecApprovalAllowedDecisions()` returns the same `["allow-once", "deny"]` for both `ask=always` (policy) and one-shot persistence (non-persistable command). Downstream renderers can't tell the difference.

Rather than introducing a new typed protocol field (`allowAlwaysUnavailableReason`) — which #97081 attempted across 65 files — we thread the existing `ask` field (already present in `ExecApprovalRequestPayload`, `ExecApprovalPendingReplyParams`, `PendingApprovalView`, Control UI request state, and the gateway/node host pipeline) through the approval-pending data pipeline so all renderers can branch on it.

The fix is at the renderer layer, not the policy decision layer. Approval policy logic is unchanged.

## Root Cause

`src/infra/exec-approvals.ts:1824` — `resolveExecApprovalAllowedDecisions()` collapses both reasons (ask=always policy and one-shot persistence) into a single return value. Nine renderers check `!allowedDecisions.includes("allow-always")` and unconditionally emit the policy message.

Introduced by: gumadeiras `ba735d015809` (generic unavailable copy)
Made visible by: jesse-merhi `c9707ab635b9` (one-shot persistence for shell-redirected commands)
Confidence: clear

## User Impact

Users with `ask=on-miss` (or any non-always policy) who run commands with shell redirection will now see:

> Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted (e.g., shell redirection or dynamic content).

Instead of the misleading:

> The effective approval policy requires approval every time, so Allow Always is unavailable.

Users with `ask=always` see the same policy message as before.

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed**: Approval prompt text blames policy when missing Allow Always is actually caused by one-shot/non-persistable command safety.

**Real environment tested**: Linux x64, Node v22.11.0, branch `fix/exec-approval-prompt-text-97069`, base commit `origin/main`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

**Reply renderer** (the function that builds gateway-to-channel approval prompts):

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "
import { buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload } from './src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts';

console.log('=== ask=always ===');
console.log(buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload({
  approvalId: 't1', approvalSlug: 's1',
  ask: 'always', allowedDecisions: ['allow-once', 'deny'],
  command: 'openclaw --version 2>&1', host: 'gateway',
}).text);

console.log('=== ask=on-miss ===');
console.log(buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload({
  approvalId: 't2', approvalSlug: 's2',
  ask: 'on-miss', allowedDecisions: ['allow-once', 'deny'],
  command: 'openclaw --version 2>&1', host: 'gateway',
}).text);
"
```

**Forwarder renderer** (real `ExecApprovalRequest` objects through the channel-forwarding path):

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "
import { buildExecApprovalRequestMessage } from './src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.ts';

var now = Date.now();
var req = {
  id: 'exec-req-1', createdAtMs: now, expiresAtMs: now + 300000,
  request: { command: 'openclaw --version 2>&1', cwd: '/home/user/project',
    host: 'gateway', agentId: 'main', security: 'allowlist' },
};

console.log('=== ask=always + allow-always excluded ===');
console.log(buildExecApprovalRequestMessage(
  { ...req, request: { ...req.request, ask: 'always' } }
));

console.log('=== ask=on-miss + allow-always excluded ===');
console.log(buildExecApprovalRequestMessage(
  { ...req, request: { ...req.request, ask: 'on-miss', unavailableDecisions: ['allow-always'] } }
));
"
```

**Evidence after fix** — real production renderer output:

**Reply renderer**:

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "..."

=== ask=always ===
The effective approval policy requires approval every time, so Allow Always is unavailable.

=== ask=on-miss (one-shot) ===
Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted (e.g., shell redirection or dynamic content).
```

**Forwarder** (real `ExecApprovalRequest` objects — same shape as the gateway production path):

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "..."

=== ask=always + allow-always excluded ===
🔒 Exec approval required
...
Allow Always is unavailable because the effective policy requires approval every time.

=== ask=on-miss + allow-always excluded ===
🔒 Exec approval required
...
Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted (e.g., shell redirection or dynamic content).
```

**Telegram approval paths** (same renderer called by `buildTelegramExecApprovalPendingPayload` and `approval-handler.runtime.ts`):

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "import ... from './src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts'; ..."

=== Telegram: ask=on-miss + allow-always excluded ===
Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted (e.g., shell redirection or dynamic content).

=== Telegram: ask=always + allow-always excluded ===
The effective approval policy requires approval every time, so Allow Always is unavailable.
```

| `ask` input | Message shown |
|---|---|
| `"always"` | "The effective approval policy requires approval every time…" (unchanged) |
| `"on-miss"` (one-shot) | "Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted…" (fixed) |
| `"off"` (full-auto with shell redirect) | "Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted…" (fixed) |
| absent / null (plugin, legacy) | "The effective approval policy requires approval every time…" (safe default) |

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/infra/exec-approval-reply.test.ts \
    src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts \
    src/plugin-sdk/approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts \
    src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts \
    src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.test.ts \
    src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts \
    src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts \
    extensions/telegram/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts \
    ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts
# all passed
```

**Observed result after fix**: All nine approval prompt renderers now show the appropriate message. Policy → policy text, one-shot → non-persistable text, absent ask → policy (safe default). Production pipeline (gateway + node hosts → ExecToolDetails → embedded handler) threads `ask` through. The node path uses `approvalDecisionAsk` (`maxAsk(hostAsk, nodeAsk)`) — keeping the reasoning consistent with `allowedDecisions` computation. Telegram native and forwarding paths pass `ask` through `buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload`. Gateway API error path uses the same `isPolicyAlways` heuristic.

Additional CI guard checks pass locally: `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:strict-smoke` (dts emit), `pnpm ui:i18n:check` (all locales clean).

**What was not tested**: Live Gateway with Telegram/Discord channel approval delivery. Telegram terminal proof above exercises the exact same renderer functions called by the Telegram plugin's `buildTelegramExecApprovalPendingPayload` and `approval-handler.runtime.ts`. Mantis automated Telegram Desktop proof will further validate on push via `pull_request_target`.

target.title: fix(memory-wiki): gracefully handle unparsable YAML frontmatter in vault scans (#96125)
target.body:
Related to #96125.

## What Problem This Solves

A single wiki page with malformed YAML frontmatter (e.g., a `sourceIds` list item containing inline `:` inside bold markdown — `**MEMORY.md line 235**:"..."`) crashes `wiki_apply`, `wiki_lint`, and any vault-wide scan via `compileMemoryWikiVault`. The `YAMLParseError` propagates from `parseWikiMarkdown` → `toWikiPageSummary` → `readPageSummaries` (which uses `errorMode: "stop"`) → entire vault compilation fails, even though the offending page has already been written successfully.

## Why This Change Was Made

The fix is placed in `toWikiPageSummary` — the scanning boundary — rather than `parseWikiMarkdown` — the parsing primitive:

- **Scanning callers** (`compile`, `lint`, `query`, `status`) all go through `toWikiPageSummary`. Catching here makes the entire vault resilient: one bad page produces a degraded summary with `frontmatterError` set, other pages compile normally.
- **Edit callers** (`apply`, `chatgpt-import`) call `parseWikiMarkdown` directly. They still throw on bad frontmatter so a write never silently overwrites existing metadata with empty fields.

If we caught in `parseWikiMarkdown` instead, edit paths would accept empty `{}` frontmatter → write back → **lose all metadata** (`id`, `sourceIds`, `claims`, etc.) on the page being edited. That is a data-loss bug.

The `splitWikiFrontmatterBlock` helper is extracted from `parseWikiMarkdown` so the catch block can still strip the frontmatter block from the raw content and preserve the body, even when YAML parsing fails.

## Root Cause

```text
parseWikiMarkdown()                                    markdown.ts:181
  → YAML.parse(match[1]) throws YAMLParseError         no try-catch
    → toWikiPageSummary() dies                         markdown.ts:620
      → readPageSummaries() errorMode:"stop" kills all read-with-concurrency.ts:62
        → compileMemoryWikiVault() throws              compile.ts:397-398
```

Introduced by: `1ee2733b2f` (vincentkoc, 2026-06-17) — initial memory-wiki extraction.  
The `YAML.parse` call has never had error handling since the module was introduced.  
Confidence: **clear**.

> **Note on existing PR**: PR #96177 identifies the same fix surface (catch in `toWikiPageSummary` + `frontmatterError` field + `invalid-frontmatter` lint). This PR adds a control-group regression test (valid YAML unaffected), an extra test assertion verifying the body survives degradation, and a more complete proof matrix produced with `node --import tsx` driving the real production `toWikiPageSummary`. The editorial call-path invariant (apply/chatgpt-import must still throw) is explicitly documented in the code comment.

## User Impact

- `wiki_apply`, `wiki_lint`, and any vault scan no longer fail because of an unrelated page elsewhere in the vault
- The broken page shows `invalid-frontmatter` in lint output with the exact parse error message, so users know which file to fix
- Other pages in the vault compile and lint normally

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed**: A wiki page with unparsable YAML frontmatter no longer crashes vault-wide scans. The degraded summary carries `frontmatterError` so lint reports the broken file. Valid pages are unaffected.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node v22.11.0, OpenClaw PR branch `fix/wiki-frontmatter-yaml-parse-96125` at `origin/main` = `68ead4dd80`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "
import { toWikiPageSummary } from './extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.ts';
// ... (3 scenarios: bad frontmatter, good frontmatter, no frontmatter)
"
```

**Evidence after fix** — matrix of input variants vs observed results:

| Input | frontmatterError | hasFrontmatter | id | title |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `sourceIds` with inline `:` in bold (the #96125 case) | `"Unexpected scalar at node end at line 4, column 17: …"` | `true` | `undefined` | `"Test Title"` |
| Valid `sourceIds: [source.alpha]` | `undefined` | `true` | `"synthesis.healthy"` | `"Healthy Page"` |
| No frontmatter block at all | `undefined` | `false` | `undefined` | `"Plain Page"` |

```console
$ pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  9 passed (9)

$ pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)
```

Full memory-wiki CI-like shard:

```console
$ pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/
 Test Files  27 passed | 2 failed (29)
      Tests  169 passed | 4 failed (173)
```

The 4 failures are all `missing node:sqlite` (Node v22.11 < 22.19 requirement) in unrelated tests (`doctor-contract-api`, `source-sync-state`). Pre-existing infrastructure gap.

**Observed result after fix**: The bad-frontmatter page produces a degraded `WikiPageSummary` with `frontmatterError` set, the page body preserved under its original title, and frontmatter-derived fields (id, sourceIds, etc.) falling back to empty. `wiki_lint` reports `invalid-frontmatter` on the offending page. Healthy pages compile and lint exactly as before.

**What was not tested**:
- Live OpenClaw gateway with a real vault containing mixed healthy/broken pages — this would require a running gateway instance. The `toWikiPageSummary` function is exercised at unit-test level with 3 input variants covering the full matrix.
- `wiki_apply` / `wiki_lint` end-to-end via gateway — these go through `compileMemoryWikiVault` → `readPageSummaries` → `toWikiPageSummary`, which is the same call chain tested in the lint integration test.

## Regression Test Plan

```console
$ pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.test.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts
```

- `toWikiPageSummary` — degrades to empty frontmatter instead of throwing (#96125)
- `toWikiPageSummary` — preserves frontmatter and body for valid YAML (#96125 control)
- `lintMemoryWikiVault` — reports unparsable frontmatter as a lint issue (#96125)

## Changes

- `extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.ts`: +25/-3 — add `frontmatterError` field to `WikiPageSummary`, extract `splitWikiFrontmatterBlock` helper, catch `YAML.parse` in `toWikiPageSummary` with degraded fallback
- `extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.ts`: +15/-0 — add `invalid-frontmatter` lint code, skip frontmatter-derived checks when `frontmatterError` is set
- `extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.test.ts`: +62/-0 — 2 new tests: degraded parse preserves body, valid YAML unaffected
- `extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts`: +41/-0 — integration test: broken page gets `invalid-frontmatter`, healthy page unaffected

target.title: [codex] Fix explicit custom web_search provider routing
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes `web_search` routing when `tools.web.search.provider` explicitly names a custom or installed web-search provider.

The agent tool wrapper was resolving late-bound runtime metadata and then allowing that runtime-selected provider to determine whether provider discovery should use the runtime registry or the bundled-only path. If the runtime metadata was stale, auto-detected, or computed before a custom provider was available in the active registry, an explicit config value could be ignored. That made an intentionally selected custom provider behave as if it were unknown or fall through to an unrelated bundled provider.

This PR makes the configured provider string the authoritative selection when it is present, and passes it through as `providerId` to the web-search runtime. The shared web-tool runtime context now also evaluates explicit config before runtime metadata when deciding whether to prefer runtime providers.

## Why This Is Needed

`tools.web.search.provider` is a user-facing explicit routing setting. When it is set, users reasonably expect that provider to be used, regardless of any prior runtime auto-detection metadata. The previous behavior inverted that precedence in the agent execution path:

- startup or secret-resolution metadata could record one provider as selected;
- the agent tool would trust that metadata first;
- provider candidate loading could then be scoped in a way that excluded the explicitly configured custom provider;
- `runWebSearch` would either throw an unknown-provider error for the configured provider or execute a different provider from the bundled set.

That is both surprising and risky. A configured provider can differ from an auto-detected provider in credentials, cost profile, data source, compliance properties, network path, or availability. Explicit config is intentionally a hard routing instruction, not a hint that stale metadata can override.

## What Changed

- `createWebSearchTool` now reads the configured `tools.web.search.provider` from the late-bound config snapshot and passes it to `runWebSearch` as `providerId`.
- `resolveWebToolRuntimeContext` now gives configured provider IDs precedence over runtime-selected metadata when deciding provider discovery mode.
- `resolveWebSearchCandidates` now reloads plugin web-search providers if an explicit provider ID was requested, runtime-provider discovery was preferred, and the active runtime registry did not contain that explicit provider.
- `docs/tools/web.md` now documents the compatibility choice: an explicit configured provider is a hard route and `web_search` fails instead of silently auto-detecting another provider if that provider cannot be loaded at runtime.

The fallback reload is intentionally narrow: it only runs for explicit provider requests that are missing from the first candidate set. Normal bundled-provider and auto-detect paths keep their existing behavior.

## Impact

Users with a configured bundled provider should see no behavior change other than stronger explicit-provider precedence. Users with installed/custom web-search providers get deterministic routing when they set `tools.web.search.provider`.

The failure mode also becomes clearer: if the explicit provider still cannot be discovered after the fallback load, the runtime reports the configured provider as unknown instead of silently drifting to another provider.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Agent `web_search` should honor an explicit `tools.web.search.provider` selection even when runtime web-search metadata is stale and points at a different provider. The intended compatibility choice is fail-closed explicit provider routing, not silent fallback to auto-detect.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw repo runtime on Linux in this isolated PR worktree, using production TypeScript modules via `pnpm exec tsx`; no gateway restart, no host config writes, and no real credentials.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a production-module terminal proof that installed a synthetic active plugin registry with `stale` and `custom` web-search providers, configured `tools.web.search.provider: "custom"`, supplied stale runtime metadata selecting `stale`, and executed the agent `web_search` tool with query `routing proof`.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the production-module proof:

```text
pnpm exec tsx -e '<production-module routing proof>'
agent_tool_explicit_custom_over_stale_runtime {"content":[{"type":"text","text":"{\n  \"query\": \"routing proof\",\n  \"provider\": \"custom\"\n}"}],"details":{"query":"routing proof","provider":"custom"}}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The agent tool returned provider `custom`, proving the explicit configured provider won over stale runtime metadata selecting `stale`.
- **What was not tested:** No live third-party search API call was made because the bug is provider routing before network execution; focused regression tests cover the runtime discovery and unknown-provider fail-closed paths.

## Validation

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/tools/web.md src/agents/tools/web-search.ts src/agents/tools/web-tool-runtime-context.ts src/agents/tools/web-tools.enabled-defaults.test.ts src/web-search/runtime.ts src/web-search/runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/tools/web-search.test.ts src/agents/tools/web-tools.enabled-defaults.test.ts src/web-search/runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json` selected `core`, `coreTests`, and `docs`.
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=1 OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled pnpm check:changed` was attempted because Testbox is not authenticated here; it completed conflict-marker, changelog attribution, dependency/patch guards, core typecheck, core test typecheck, core lint, runtime sidecar loader, and runtime import-cycle checks, then left an idle node process with no active child work and was stopped to avoid leaving it behind.
- `pnpm check:docs`

Testbox was attempted first but is unavailable in this environment:

```text
blacksmith testbox warmup ci-check-testbox.yml --ref main --idle-timeout 90
blacksmith testbox list
# Error: not authenticated — run 'blacksmith auth login' first
```

Known unrelated broad-check note: `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` currently reports `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` hash drift even though this branch has no generated file changes and does not touch Plugin SDK exports.

target.title: refactor: flip sessions and transcripts to sqlite storage
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Completes the session/transcript storage flip tracked by openclaw/openclaw#88838 by making per-agent SQLite the canonical runtime store for session metadata and transcript events. Legacy `sessions.json` and active transcript JSONL files are now doctor import inputs or archive artifacts, not runtime fallback state.

## Why This Change Was Made

The accessor seams from the earlier work made a single storage flip practical. Shipping a mixed file/SQLite runtime would let session metadata and transcript rows drift, so this PR moves import, runtime access, retention, SDK cleanup, and ratchets together behind the permanent accessor boundary.

## User Impact

Existing legacy session stores can be migrated non-destructively through doctor dry-run/import/validate/inspect flows. Runtime session creation, resume, transcript append, compaction, and maintenance use SQLite rows; retention still exports archived JSONL artifacts before deleting rows so support and archive workflows keep inspectable artifacts.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/check-session-accessor-boundary.mjs`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/sessions/session-accessor.test.ts src/config/sessions/targets.test.ts src/commands/doctor-session-sqlite.test.ts test/scripts/check-session-accessor-boundary.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.test.ts src/config/sessions/session-accessor.conformance.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.session-lifecycle-mutation.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/session-entry-projection.contract.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts`

target.title: feat(agents): per-agent outbound A2A allowlist override
target.body:
## Summary

- Add `agents.list[].tools.agentToAgent.allow` to control which agents a specific agent may contact via session tools (`sessions_send`, `sessions_list`, `sessions_history`, `session_status`)
- When unset, the agent falls back to the global `tools.agentToAgent.allow` list; when set to explicit IDs, only those targets are reachable; `[]` isolates the agent from all cross-agent access
- The global `tools.agentToAgent.allow` continues to act as a participation gate (both requester and target must match it) before per-agent outbound rules are evaluated
- Outbound-only semantics: only the requester's override is checked, not the target's
- Replace `matchesAllow` with `matchesGlobalParticipation` and add `evaluateAccess()` returning a structured `AgentToAgentDenyReason` so all call sites produce context-aware error messages via `formatAgentToAgentAccessError()`
- Scrub deleted agent references from per-agent outbound allowlists in `pruneAgentConfig()`

### Example config (hub-spoke pattern)

```json
{
  "tools": {
    "agentToAgent": { "enabled": true, "allow": ["*"] }
  },
  "agents": {
    "list": [
      { "id": "david",  "tools": { "agentToAgent": { "allow": ["*"] } } },
      { "id": "spoke",  "tools": { "agentToAgent": { "allow": ["david"] } } },
      { "id": "kelly",  "tools": { "agentToAgent": { "allow": ["david"] } } }
    ]
  }
}
```

spoke→david ✅ | spoke→kelly ❌ | david→anyone ✅

## Test plan

- [x] `sessions-access.test.ts`: per-agent override, deny-all `[]`, global gate, target override ignored (outbound-only), fallback to global when unset
- [x] `sessions.test.ts`: per-agent outbound deny during label lookup in `sessions_send`
- [x] `openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts`: per-agent outbound deny for cross-agent `session_status`
- [x] `agents.test.ts`: `pruneAgentConfig` scrubs deleted agent from per-agent allowlists
- [x] Build passes
- [x] Manual: configure hub-spoke agents (david/spoke/kelly), verify per-agent outbound rules — see `Real behavior proof` section below

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: per-agent outbound `agentToAgent.allow` correctly gates cross-agent session tool access. Hub-spoke topology used: `david` (hub, `allow=["*"]`), `spoke` (allow=`["david"]`), `kelly` (allow=`["david"]`). Validates the new `evaluateAccess()` decision pipeline reaches the documented per-agent outbound semantics, with the structured `per_agent_outbound` deny reason surfaced via `formatAgentToAgentAccessError()`.

- **Real environment tested**: Ubuntu 22.04 (kernel 5.15), Node 22.22.0, pnpm 10. Branch HEAD `55819f6f43` of `repair/pr-39102-a2a-refresh` (this PR rebased on current main). Live invocation of the production export `createAgentToAgentPolicy` from `src/plugin-sdk/session-visibility.ts` — no mocks, no test runner, the same code path that backs `sessions_send`, `sessions_list`, `sessions_history`, and `session_status` at runtime.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

  1. Author a hub-spoke `OpenClawConfig` matching the docs example (david hub + spoke + kelly).
  2. Build `policy = createAgentToAgentPolicy(cfg)`.
  3. Call `policy.evaluateAccess(requester, target)` for the five edge pairs.
  4. Run with: `pnpm tsx proof-hub-spoke.ts` (the script imports the worktree source directly so the running code is the patched export).

- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output, copied verbatim from `node` stdout via `tsx`.

  ```
  # per-agent A2A allowlist — hub-spoke proof
  # config: david=hub (allow *), spoke=spoke (allow david), kelly=spoke (allow david)

  [ok ] spoke -> david: ALLOWED
  [ok ] spoke -> kelly: DENIED (reason=per_agent_outbound)
  [ok ] david -> kelly: ALLOWED
  [ok ] david -> spoke: ALLOWED
  [ok ] kelly -> spoke: DENIED (reason=per_agent_outbound)

  all cases match expected behavior
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: all five real invocations matched the documented hub-spoke semantics. Cross-spoke calls (`spoke -> kelly`, `kelly -> spoke`) were denied with the structured `per_agent_outbound` reason code from `AgentToAgentDecision`. Hub-rooted calls (`david -> spoke`, `david -> kelly`) and spoke-to-hub (`spoke -> david`) were allowed. Process exit code: `0`. Console output above is the literal stdout, no post-processing.

- **What was not tested**: end-to-end IPC roundtrip through the live `sessions_send` agent process pair (single-machine ACP transport). The four session call sites all delegate the access decision to the single `evaluateAccess()` policy proven above, and the wiring of each call site is covered by the unit tests already listed in the Test plan.

target.title: fix(agents): drop throttled exec update events
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: exec live output throttling capped detailed payloads, but throttled `tool_execution_update` events still emitted generic tool/item updates with no new user-visible payload.
- Solution: make throttled exec updates a true no-op until the next allowed live update or final result.
- What changed: `handleToolExecutionUpdate` now returns before emitting generic exec update traffic when the live exec update throttle suppresses the detailed output, with regression coverage for global agent events and `onAgentEvent` emissions.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): final exec result events, command completion items, non-exec tool updates, and the existing live exec output size cap are unchanged.

## Motivation

- #84850 captured a 60s gateway sample with average CPU at 83.66%, 42/60 samples at or above 100% CPU, and 0.00 kB/s average read I/O during an embedded exec tool run. Dropping redundant throttled update events reduces avoidable gateway projection/broadcast work in that hot path.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #84850
- Related #78645
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Gateway CPU saturation from #84850 during embedded exec tool output. Throttled exec updates should not continue producing generic tool/item event traffic when they carry no new visible output.
- Real environment tested: Local Linux OpenClaw worktree on Node 24, using the repo Vitest wrapper plus the redacted pidstat/gateway log evidence from the affected run.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts`, `git diff --check`, and `codex --dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox --sandbox danger-full-access review --uncommitted`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied terminal capture from the patched worktree:

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts

Test Files  2 passed (2)
     Tests  76 passed (76)

$ git diff --check
(no output; exit 0)

$ codex --dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox --sandbox danger-full-access review --uncommitted
No discrete correctness issues were found in the current local changes.
```

- Observed result after fix: The regression test proves the first exec live update still emits visible output, while the next update inside the throttle window emits no additional global agent events and no additional `onAgentEvent` traffic. Final result handling remains covered by the existing exec result tests.
- What was not tested: I did not rerun the private 60s live CPU-saturation gateway session because the original setup/session details are local and redacted; the fix was verified at the exec tool update seam that emits the redundant events.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Redacted source evidence from #84850:

```text
pidstat summary:
rows=60
avg_cpu=83.66
avg_usr=79.42
avg_sys=4.25
avg_rd=0.00
cpu_ge_100_count=42
max_cpu=190.0

redacted gateway correlation:
2026-05-21T05:07:47.187+00:00 agent/embedded embedded run tool start: runId=[redacted run id] tool=exec toolCallId=REDACTED
2026-05-21T05:08:04.330+00:00 agent/embedded embedded run tool end: runId=[redacted run id] tool=exec toolCallId=REDACTED
```

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the live exec throttle suppressed detailed output but still allowed generic exec update item/tool events to be emitted for every throttled update.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing coverage checked output truncation/throttling but did not assert that suppressed updates stop all generic event emission.
- Contributing context (if known): #78645 bounded live exec output payloads, but redundant event emission could still keep gateway event projection hot during high-volume exec output.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: a second exec update inside `LIVE_EXEC_UPDATE_MIN_INTERVAL_MS` emits no global agent events and no `onAgentEvent` traffic.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the redundant CPU work starts at the embedded tool update handler before gateway websocket fanout sees the event.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing exec output throttling tests cover payload capping but not generic event suppression.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Exec tool output updates inside the live update throttle window are dropped completely instead of sending generic no-payload progress events. Start, allowed live update, and final result events are preserved.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[exec update inside throttle] -> [generic tool/item events] -> [gateway projection/broadcast work]

After:
[exec update inside throttle] -> [no emitted event] -> [next allowed update or final result closes state]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 24 local worktree
- Model/provider: NOT_ENOUGH_INFO for the original runtime sample; tests do not require provider credentials.
- Integration/channel (if any): Embedded exec tool event handling
- Relevant config (redacted): Original private session details were redacted.

### Steps

1. Run `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts`.
2. Run `git diff --check`.
3. Run `codex --dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox --sandbox danger-full-access review --uncommitted`.

### Expected

- First exec live update emits visible command output.
- A throttled follow-up exec update emits no generic event traffic.
- Final exec result behavior remains unchanged.

### Actual

- Targeted wrapper passed: 2 files, 76 tests.
- `git diff --check` passed with no output.
- Autoreview found no discrete correctness issues.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [x] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: allowed exec live update still emits output; throttled exec update emits no global or local agent event traffic; final exec output/result tests still pass.
- Edge cases checked: existing large-output truncation and final output redaction coverage still passes in the same target file.
- What you did **not** verify: private live CPU-saturation rerun from the original affected setup.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Suppressing throttled updates could hide command progress between allowed live updates.
  - Mitigation: the first allowed update and final command result remain emitted; only no-payload updates inside the existing throttle window are dropped.

target.title: Run memory flush before preflight compaction
target.body:
Fixes #84695.

## Summary

- run automatic memory flush before preflight compaction in `runReplyAgent`
- keep visible memory-flush errors on the same pre-run return path before the main reply
- add regression coverage proving memory flush is invoked before preflight compaction

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `runReplyAgent` must run the memory-flush phase before the preflight-compaction phase so durable memory has a chance to run before aggressive transcript reduction.
- **Real environment tested:** PR head `ccadfb5cbf03bd5fc7352df49b1726afff1bfa8e`, worktree `/Users/andy/openclaw-84792`, local Node `v24.15.0`, diagnostics timeline enabled via `OPENCLAW_DIAGNOSTICS=1` and `OPENCLAW_DIAGNOSTICS_TIMELINE_PATH`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Rebased the PR onto current `upstream/main`, then ran the patched `runReplyAgent` path through the direct runtime test with diagnostics timeline enabled and filtered the emitted timeline for `reply.memory_flush` and `reply.preflight_compaction` spans.

```sh
OPENCLAW_DIAGNOSTICS_TIMELINE_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-84792-current-head-timeline.jsonl \

```

target.title: fix(acpx): scrub provider credential env from ACP harness spawns
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `sessions_spawn` with `runtime:"acp"` + `agentId:"claude"` fails immediately with `AcpRuntimeError: Internal error: Invalid API key · code=ACP_TURN_FAILED`.
- **Solution:** Strip provider-credential env vars from the ACP harness launch command, keyed on the agent id, so each harness uses its own auth instead of inheriting the gateway's creds.
- **What changed:** A table-driven per-harness scrub merged into the existing acpx command decoration as an `env -u <NAME>` prefix; new `acp.scrubProviderEnv` config knob (default `true`).
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** The gateway's own auth/model-call path is untouched; the OpenClaw ACP bridge keeps the full env; reuse identity stays the raw command (no session-reuse changes); no blanket env stripping — only named provider-credential vars.

## Motivation

Operators set `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in `~/.openclaw/.env` so the gateway can authenticate its own model calls. The value may be a standard key **or** an OAuth access token (`sk-ant-oat01-…`) — Anthropic's `/v1/messages` accepts both, so the gateway works either way. But Claude Code's CLI auth path **rejects** an OAuth token supplied via `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` because it expects its own Keychain OAuth flow. Since an ACP harness inherits the gateway's environment, the spawned `claude` child sees `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, tries to use it, and dies with "Invalid API key" before doing any work.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** ACP spawn of `agentId:"claude"` fails with `Invalid API key`.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS; Claude Code authenticated via Keychain OAuth; `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`. The reporter confirmed the diagnosis on this setup (see below). **Full disclosure:** I have not yet re-run a live `sessions_spawn` against a gateway built from this branch — the after-fix evidence below proves the env-strip *mechanism* and the resolved command, via the package's own tests plus a real child process. A live end-to-end run by someone with the failing setup is still recommended before merge.
- **Exact steps/commands run after this patch:**
  - `pnpm` acpx suite: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-acpx.config.ts` → **146 passed**
  - Typecheck: `tsgo:core`, `tsgo:extensions`, `tsgo:extensions:test` → clean
  - Config generators: `config:schema:check`, `config:docs:check` → ok
- **Evidence after fix (real child process):** a POSIX test builds the decorated command and runs it through a shell with the creds set in the parent env, then reads them back in the child:
  ```
  parent env: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-oat01-…, ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN=…, OPENCLAW_KEEPME=still-here
  command:    env -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY -u ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN node -e '…print the three vars…'
  child sees: { a: null, t: null, keep: "still-here" }
  ```
  i.e. the two credentials are gone from the child while unrelated vars survive.
- **Observed result after fix:** harness launch command for `claude` becomes `env -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY -u ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN … node ".../claude-agent-acp-wrapper.mjs" …`; the child no longer inherits the colliding credential.
- **What was not tested:** a live `sessions_spawn runtime:"acp" agentId:"claude"` against a gateway running this exact build (failing session keys exist on the reporter's machine).
- **Before evidence:** reporter's repro — `claude --print 'ping'` fails (inherits `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`); `env -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY claude --print 'ping'` works. Renaming the var to `OPENCLAW_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in `.env` also fixes ACP spawns, confirming the collision is the cause.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** Auth-path collision. The gateway's `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` (possibly an OAuth token) is inherited by the spawned Claude Code harness, whose CLI refuses OAuth tokens supplied through that env var. The real spawn happens in the acpx backend (`extensions/acpx/`), which launches the harness from a command string; the gateway's only lever over the child env is that string.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No isolation of provider credentials between the gateway and the ACP harnesses it spawns; harnesses silently inherited gateway creds.
- **Contributing context:** Each harness (claude, codex, gemini, cursor, copilot, droid, opencode) has its own auth mechanism, so none should inherit OpenClaw's provider creds.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `extensions/acpx/src/provider-env-scrub.test.ts`, `extensions/acpx/src/process-lease.test.ts` (incl. real child-process strip), `extensions/acpx/src/runtime.provider-env-scrub.test.ts` (scoped-registry wiring + classification).
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** resolving the `claude` harness command yields an `env -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY -u ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN` prefix; codex/gemini strip their providers; the openclaw bridge is untouched; `acp.scrubProviderEnv:false` disables it; command classification still works through the `env -u` prefix.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** the scrub is a pure, table-driven function plus a deterministic command decoration — unit-coverable without a live agent.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- ACP harnesses (claude/codex/gemini/…) no longer inherit the gateway's provider credentials; each falls back to its own auth.
- New config `acp.scrubProviderEnv` (boolean, **default `true`**). Set `false` to restore the old inherit-everything behavior.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
sessions_spawn(acp, claude) -> spawn "node claude-agent-acp-wrapper.mjs"
  child env INHERITS ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (gateway's, may be OAuth token)
  -> Claude Code reads it -> "Invalid API key" -> ACP_TURN_FAILED

After:
sessions_spawn(acp, claude) -> spawn "env -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY -u ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN node claude-agent-acp-wrapper.mjs"
  child env has NO ANTHROPIC_* -> Claude Code uses its own Keychain OAuth -> works
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **Yes** — fewer secrets reach subprocesses: provider credentials are removed from the spawned harness environment (a hardening; reduces credential leakage into third-party harnesses).
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **Yes (minor)** — the harness command string gains a leading `env -u <NAME>` prefix. Conservative: only named provider-credential vars are unset; `unwrapEnvCommand` was extended so command classification and the process reaper still match.
- Data access scope changed? **No**
- Risk + mitigation: see Risks below.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw gateway + acpx backend
- Model/provider: Anthropic (Claude Code harness over ACP)
- Integration/channel: ACP `sessions_spawn`
- Relevant config (redacted): `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` set in `~/.openclaw/.env` (value is an OAuth token in the reporter's case)

### Steps

1. With `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` set in `~/.openclaw/.env`, run `sessions_spawn` with `runtime:"acp"`, `agentId:"claude"`.
2. Observe failure (before this patch).
3. With this patch, repeat — the harness launches with provider creds stripped.

### Expected

- The Claude Code harness starts and uses its own Keychain OAuth.

### Actual (before fix)

- `AcpRuntimeError: Internal error: Invalid API key · code=ACP_TURN_FAILED`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (TDD: tests written red, then green; see test files)
- [x] Trace/log snippets (child-process strip output above)
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** new + existing acpx suite (146 passing) including a real child-process test proving the credential is absent from the child env; scoped-registry produces the scrubbed command for claude/codex/gemini and leaves the openclaw bridge untouched; `acp.scrubProviderEnv:false` disables it; classification tolerates the `env -u` prefix; core+extensions typecheck and lint clean; regenerated config schema/doc baseline checks pass.
- **Edge cases checked:** unknown harness id (strips the union of known provider creds); Windows no-op (`env -u` not portable); de-duped credential names; persistent-session reuse unaffected (reuse identity is the raw command).
- **What I did NOT verify:** a live `sessions_spawn runtime:"acp" agentId:"claude"` against a gateway built from this branch.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Mostly** — harness child env changes by design (the fix). Any setup that *relied* on a harness inheriting a gateway provider cred can restore the old behavior with `acp.scrubProviderEnv: false`.
- Config/env changes? **Yes** — new optional `acp.scrubProviderEnv` (default `true`).
- Migration needed? **No.**

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** A harness that legitimately depended on inheriting a gateway provider credential loses it.
  - **Mitigation:** Per-harness table is conservative (only that harness's own provider creds for known harnesses); the `openclaw` bridge is exempt; global opt-out via `acp.scrubProviderEnv: false`.
- **Risk:** The `env -u` prefix could confuse command classification / process cleanup.
  - **Mitigation:** `unwrapEnvCommand` extended to skip `-u NAME`; covered by classification tests; reuse/cleanup use the raw stable command, which is unchanged.

target.title: feat(secrets): scan backup files for plaintext provider apiKey values
target.body:
## Summary

Adds backup file secret scanning to the `openclaw secrets audit` command. This addresses a security gap identified in issue #11829 where old backup files (e.g., `models.json.20260501.bak`, `openclaw.json.old`) may retain plaintext provider API keys even after the active config is sanitized.

## Changes

- **storage-scan.ts**: New `listKnownSecretFileBackups()` function that discovers backup files in the config directory and agents directory matching patterns like `models.json.*`, `openclaw.json.*`, `*.bak`, `*.backup`, `*.old`
- **audit.ts**: New `collectBackupResidue()` function that scans backup files for plaintext `apiKey` values and reports them as `LEGACY_RESIDUE` findings
- **audit.test.ts**: 3 test cases covering:
  - Detecting plaintext apiKey in `.bak` files
  - Detecting plaintext apiKey in `.old` files  
  - Not flagging non-secret markers (env var names) in backup files

Part of #11829 - Protecting API Keys from Agent Access

target.title: fix(agents): surface user-visible error when embedded session is stuck or overflows context (#84536)
target.body:
Fixes #84536.

## Problem

Preemptive context overflow checks (`shouldPreemptivelyCompactBeforePrompt`) fire during tool loops in embedded agent sessions. The session ends with `embedded_run_agent_end` + error, but **no error message is delivered to the channel**. Sessions remain stuck in "processing" state for hours until manual gateway restart.

## Root Causes

### Bug 1: Dead-code overflow fallback in `agent-runner-execution.ts`

The `!hasPayloadText` guard on the context overflow fallback at the end of `runAgentTurnWithFallback` made the branch unreachable in the common terminal-overflow path. `run.ts` always includes an error payload when it reaches the terminal overflow return, so `hasPayloadText` was always `true` and this fallback never fired.

When the session was genuinely stuck (aborted by stuck-session recovery after hours), the abort result had *empty* payloads and `meta.error` unset — so neither the early check nor this fallback fired, and the user saw nothing.

**Fix:** Remove the `!hasPayloadText` guard. The early check at line ~490 already returns for sessions that can be reset; by the time we reach this fallback, the reset failed or was unavailable, so we should always surface the overflow error.

### Bug 2: Stuck-session recovery produces no user notification

`recoverStuckDiagnosticSession` (triggered after `stuckSessionWarnMs` of a session in processing state) calls `abortAndDrainEmbeddedPiRun` and releases the lane — but the aborted run returns with empty payloads and no error in `meta`, so the user receives no message.

**Fix:** Thread the abort reason (`"stuck_recovery"`) through:
1. `abortAndDrainEmbeddedPiRun` → `handle.abort(reason)` (when reason is provided)
2. `handle.abort(reason)` → `runAbortController.abort(reason)` (abort signal now carries reason)
3. `runAbortController.signal.reason` → `EmbeddedRunAttemptResult.abortReason`
4. `attempt.abortReason === "stuck_recovery"` in `run.ts` → synthesize a user-visible error payload

The synthesized payload delivers: *"⚠️ Your session was stuck and has been automatically recovered. Please try again."*

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts` | Remove `!hasPayloadText` dead guard; always surface overflow error |
| `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run-state.ts` | `EmbeddedPiQueueHandle.abort: (reason?: unknown) => void` |
| `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/runs.ts` | Pass `params.reason` to `handle.abort(reason)` in `abortAndDrainEmbeddedPiRun` |
| `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/types.ts` | Add `abortReason?: string` to `EmbeddedRunAttemptResult` |
| `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts` | Read `runAbortController.signal.reason` and expose as `abortReason` |
| `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts` | Synthesize "session was stuck" payload when `abortReason === "stuck_recovery"` |

## Design Notes

- No compaction policy or threshold changes
- No new user-facing configuration
- Abort reason threading uses the standard `AbortController.abort(reason)` / `signal.reason` pattern already present in the codebase (see `sessions_yield` abort)
- `stuckRecoveryPayload` is only synthesized when `payloadsForTerminalPath` is empty — normal completions and overflow terminal paths keep their existing payloads
- `livenessState` is set to `"blocked"` for stuck-recovery aborts to match the existing convention for error-terminal paths

## Acceptance criteria

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.overflow-compaction.loop.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.lifecycle.test.ts
```

---

## Fix commit (f4e723e0)

**Root cause fix:** `queueHandle.abort` was wired as `abort: abortRun` where `abortRun(isTimeout = false, reason?)` takes the timeout flag as first arg. Calling `handle.abort("stuck_recovery")` passed `"stuck_recovery"` into the `isTimeout` slot (truthy → `timedOut = true`), leaving `reason = undefined`. The abort signal received `makeTimeoutAbortReason()` instead of `"stuck_recovery"`, so `attempt.abortReason` was never `"stuck_recovery"` and the stuck-recovery payload was never synthesized.

**Fix:** `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts:3314`
```diff
-  abort: abortRun,
+  abort: (reason?: unknown) => abortRun(false, reason),
```

This matches the updated `EmbeddedPiQueueHandle` type (`abort: (reason?: unknown) => void`) and ensures `"stuck_recovery"` flows correctly to `runAbortController.abort(reason)` → `signal.reason` → `abortReason` → `isStuckRecoveryAbort` → user-visible recovery payload. ✓

**Tests added:** `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/runs.test.ts` — asserts that when `abortAndDrainEmbeddedPiRun({ reason: "stuck_recovery" })` is called, `handle.abort` receives `"stuck_recovery"` as the reason (not isTimeout).

All three acceptance criteria test commands pass.

target.title: Audit secret-bearing backup files
target.body:
## Summary
- extend `secrets audit` to scan sibling backup files for `.env`, `auth-profiles.json`, and generated `models.json`
- detect plaintext credential residue in `.bak` / `.bak.*` backup naming patterns without resolving historical backup-only SecretRefs
- keep `openclaw.json` config backup scanning out of this PR so it can remain aligned with #83275's config artifact scanner
- add coverage for env, auth profile, models backup residues, numbered backup files, and backup-only SecretRef cases

## Context
This addresses a remaining API-key exposure surface discussed in #11829: historical backup files can retain plaintext credentials even after users migrate active config to SecretRefs.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw secrets audit --json` now scans secret-bearing backup siblings for `.env`, `auth-profiles.json`, and generated `models.json`, and reports plaintext residues from those backup files. It does not scan `openclaw.json` config backups in this PR because #83275 owns that config-artifact surface.

**Real environment tested:** Local source checkout on macOS, Node.js v25.6.1, pnpm 11.1.0 via `npx pnpm@11.1.0`. The run used a temporary OpenClaw state directory with only redacted dummy values.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
1. Created a temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` with active `openclaw.json`, empty `auth-profiles.json`, and generated `models.json`.
2. Added redacted backup files: `.env.bak.1`, `auth-profiles.json.bak.1`, and `models.json.bak.1`.
3. Ran:

```bash
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$TMP/state" \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$TMP/state/openclaw.json" \
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-redacted-active \
npx pnpm@11.1.0 --silent openclaw secrets audit --json
```

**Evidence after fix:** Redacted console output from the real CLI run:

```json
{
  "status": "findings",
  "summary": {
    "plaintextCount": 3,
    "unresolvedRefCount": 0,
    "shadowedRefCount": 1,
    "legacyResidueCount": 0
  },
  "filesScanned": [
    "$TMP/state/.env.bak.1",
    "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/auth-profiles.json",
    "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/auth-profiles.json.bak.1",
    "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/models.json",
    "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/models.json.bak.1",
    "$TMP/state/openclaw.json"
  ],
  "backupFindings": [
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$TMP/state/.env.bak.1",
      "jsonPath": "$env.OPENAI_API_KEY",
      "message": "Potential secret found in .env (OPENAI_API_KEY)."
    },
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/auth-profiles.json.bak.1",
      "jsonPath": "profiles.openai:default.key",
      "message": "Auth profile API key is stored as plaintext."
    },
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/models.json.bak.1",
      "jsonPath": "providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "models.json provider apiKey is stored as plaintext."
    }
  ]
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The CLI returned `status: findings`, scanned all three backup files, and reported three backup plaintext findings with `unresolvedRefCount: 0`.

**What was not tested:** I did not test real user credentials, live providers, or `openclaw.json` config backup artifacts. The config backup artifact surface is intentionally left to #83275.

## Test
- `npx pnpm@11.1.0 vitest run src/secrets/audit.test.ts` -> 26 passed
- `npx pnpm@11.1.0 exec oxfmt --check src/secrets/storage-scan.ts src/secrets/audit.ts src/secrets/audit.test.ts`
- `npx pnpm@11.1.0 exec oxlint src/secrets/storage-scan.ts src/secrets/audit.ts src/secrets/audit.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: fix(discord): guard message-tool-only final delivery
target.body:
## Summary

Discord `message_tool_only` turns can currently end without any visible source-channel delivery when the model does not call the `message` tool:

- terminal `NO_REPLY` despite an owed visible status/result;
- empty assistant final after tool work;
- substantive normal final text that is then suppressed by message-tool-only source reply policy.

Prompt/tool-description guidance helps, but it is advisory. This adds a narrow runtime guard at the `runReplyAgent` result/suppression boundary.

## What changed

- Detect Discord `sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "message_tool_only"` turns with no committed successful message-tool/media/target delivery evidence.
- For substantive final payloads, mark them with existing `deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression` metadata so dispatch can surface them instead of silently suppressing them.
- For `NO_REPLY` / empty-final paths, synthesize a visible guard/error payload, also marked for source delivery.
- For non-mentioned Discord threads/channels where silent replies are otherwise allowed, keep pure ambient/no-tool `NO_REPLY` quiet but treat tool activity, block progress, cron side effects, or substantive final text as response-owed evidence.
- Also cover Discord tool/progress activity followed by empty/`NO_REPLY` finals when `sourceReplyDeliveryMode` is not `message_tool_only`; real thread traces showed that path can occur while visible progress/tool chatter is emitted but no final/status answer is delivered.
- Keep privacy/silence boundaries:
  - skip heartbeats;
  - skip `room_event` strict/background turns;
  - skip turns with committed message-tool delivery evidence;
  - keep unaddressed group/channel silence quiet when empty replies are allowed.

## Related context

This complements, rather than replaces, nearby work:

- #84022 — current Discord transcript-only/suppressed final class.
- #84328 — nearby normal-text fallback when the model skips the message tool (closed unmerged at PR preparation time).
- #84232 — best-effort for implicit tool-only source replies / `reconcileUnknownSend` path (open at PR preparation time).
- #82609 — merged strict `message_tool` policy baseline.
- #80949 — earlier diagnostic/recovery approach (closed unmerged); this PR uses a targeted runtime delivery guard instead.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Discord `message_tool_only` turns should no longer end with no visible source-channel reply when a directly addressed turn produces `NO_REPLY`, an empty final, or normal final text without a successful `message.send`.
- **Real setup tested**: Real OpenClaw deployment on Linux/arm64, installed package `OpenClaw 2026.5.12 (f066dd2)`, with the same runtime-guard logic from this PR ported to the installed compiled runtime. The deployment was restarted after applying the patch so the running gateway loaded the updated runtime.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Applied the PR-shaped runtime guard to the installed runtime bundle.
  2. Restarted the real gateway process.
  3. Ran the updated hotfix verifier and syntax check against the installed runtime bundle after the panel-reviewed visible-reply-evidence revision.
  4. Captured a follow-up real deployment regression for non-mentioned Discord thread/channel tool work followed by `NO_REPLY` / empty final, then ported the response-owed guard to source and to the installed runtime bundle on disk.
  5. Ran the response-owed hotfix verifier and `node --check` against the installed runtime bundle.
  6. Confirmed the gateway process start time was after the earlier visible-evidence patch was applied; the response-owed disk hotfix still requires a later gateway restart to become active in the running gateway.
- **Evidence after fix**: Redacted terminal output from the real deployment:

```text
$ openclaw --version

$ ps -eo pid,lstart,cmd | grep '[n]ode .*openclaw.*gateway'

$ node <proof-script> --verify

    "softerGuardText": true,
    "selectiveHelper": true,
    "skipsBlockedPayloads": true,
    "preservesAlreadyMarkedReentry": true,
    "noBroadMap": true,
    "guardUsesVisibleEvidence": true,
    "ignoresToolProgressEvidence": true,
    "excludesCronAddsFromGuardEvidence": true,
    "traceSynthesized": true,
    "traceMarking": true,
    "drainComment": true,
    "finalMark": true,
    "substantiveFinalHelper": true,

    "responseActivityFromTools": true,

    "responseActivityFromCron": true,
    "ok": true
  }

$ node --check <openclaw-install>/dist/agent-runner.runtime-<hash>.js

```

- **Observed result after fix**: After the earlier restart, the real deployment was running with the PR-shaped runtime guard loaded. The verifier confirmed the guard text, selective first-substantive-payload marking, `beforeAgentRunBlocked` skip, re-entry preservation for already-marked payloads, guard trace logging, pending-tool-drain ordering comment, and the narrower visible-reply evidence split. A follow-up response-owed disk hotfix verifier confirmed the non-mentioned Discord thread/channel coverage. A later real trace showed an additional gap where `sourceReplyDeliveryMode` was not `message_tool_only` and `toolSummary` was absent even though visible tool/progress chatter existed; the latest source update adds a regression test for that path and treats successful progress/side-effect activity as response-owed activity while still keeping pure ambient/no-tool silence quiet.
- **What was not tested**: I did not run the upstream PR branch itself as the production gateway. The source PR head is covered by the targeted tests above; the real-environment proof covers the equivalent compiled runtime seam after applying the same guard logic to an installed deployment. The deployment proof is used as real-behavior evidence for the guard seam; source tests cover the latest refined predicate. Local installed-runtime hotfixes are temporary validation artifacts and may require a separate restart to match the latest source head exactly.


### Latest v3 refinement

After applying the response-owed variant in a real deployment, follow-up Discord traces exposed an additional path: the source reply mode was not `message_tool_only`, but the run still emitted tool/progress chatter and then ended with an empty final. The latest source head handles that path while avoiding a false positive where block-streamed content already delivered a substantive automatic reply:

- Discord + no committed visible final evidence + tool/progress activity + empty/`NO_REPLY` final ⇒ guard payload.
- Non-`message_tool_only` substantive final payloads stay on the normal automatic-delivery path.
- `message_tool_only` substantive finals are still marked for source delivery.
- Room events, explicit silent-expected turns, committed `message.send` evidence, and approval prompts still skip the guard.

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix: recover terminal session status on visible inbound turns
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #86827 — group chat sessions stuck in `failed` status silently drop all subsequent messages.

When a group chat session enters a terminal status (`failed`, `timeout`, or `killed`), the reply dispatch path returns a successful no-op (`queuedFinal: false`, zero dispatch counts) without starting an AI turn or delivering any user-visible error. All subsequent messages are silently discarded until a manual `/new` reset.

## Root Cause

Three independent mechanisms conspire to create the silent drop:

1. **Session state persistence**: `initSessionState()` reuses the existing session entry with its terminal `status` field intact, passing it downstream unchanged.
2. **Reply operation locking**: A stale `ReplyOperation` from the failed run remains registered in `replyRunRegistry`, causing `admitReplyTurn()` to return `skipped` with reason `active-run`.
3. **No fallback or diagnostic**: The `skipped` admission produces a valid dispatch result with zero counts, and no warning is logged — the message simply vanishes.

## Changes

### 1. Auto-recover terminal sessions (`session.ts`)

When a **visible** (non-cron/heartbeat) inbound message hits a session with recoverable terminal status, automatically clear the stale lifecycle fields (`status`, `startedAt`, `endedAt`, `runtimeMs`, `abortedLastRun`) while **preserving the `sessionId` and transcript continuity** — no session rotation.

### 2. Clear stale reply operations with race guard (`dispatch-from-config.ts`)

When `admitReplyTurn()` returns `skipped` with reason `active-run` but the persisted session is in terminal status, force-clear the stale reply operation via `forceClearReplyRunBySessionId()` and retry admission **once**. This breaks the deadlock without risking infinite retry loops.

**Concurrent recovery guard**: After a recovery turn clears the proven stale leftover and admits its own fresh operation, it is marked with a `terminalRecovery` flag. If a second visible message arrives against the same terminal snapshot before the first recovery clears the store state, the force-clear branch now skips operations marked as in-flight terminal recoveries. This prevents a sibling recovery turn from killing a fresh recovery that is already in flight, letting concurrent turns fall through to normal busy/wait handling instead.

### 3. Agent API path (`agent.ts`)

Mirror the same terminal recovery logic in the agent API dispatch path so agent API requests also clear terminal state for visible requests.

### 4. Diagnostics (`kernel.ts`, `types.ts`)

Add a structured warning log when a visible channel turn dispatch completes with `queuedFinal: false` and zero tool/block/final counts — a sentinel for future silent message drops. Excludes system turns (heartbeat/cron/exec) and observe-only admissions.

## Real behavior proof

Captured by driving the **real** runtime modules (`dispatchReplyFromConfig`, `initSessionState`, `reply-run-registry`) with verbose logging on, **no vitest and no module mocks** — only the production parameters the Gateway supplies plus a real on-disk JSON session store. A before/after contrast was produced by running the identical driver in a pre-fix worktree.

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A group-chat session left in a terminal lifecycle status (`failed`/`timeout`/`killed`) silently drops every later message — the stale lifecycle entry plus a leftover "active" reply operation block turn admission, so a subsequent visible inbound message gets no AI turn and no reply (#86827).
- **Real environment tested**: Compiled branch source at HEAD `ac52eb0b24`; macOS 15.3.1 (Darwin 24.3.0, arm64), Node `v24.10.0`. Product built with `pnpm build:docker` (exit 0); the recovery log string is present in compiled `dist/`. Evidence captured via `tsx` driving the real in-process dispatch/session/registry path. Before/after contrast run at pre-fix commit `8de37e1ce4` (parent of the first PR commit).
- **Failing proof before fix** (`8de37e1ce4`, identical driver + input): real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible group turn on a `status="failed"` session with a stale running reply op —
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  [dispatch] dispatch TIMED-OUT (no reply within 8s): visible turn stuck behind the stale terminal reply op -> SILENT DROP
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = null
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = true
  ```
  And real `initSessionState()` against an on-disk store: `RESULT: STILL-TERMINAL — session entry remains status=failed after a visible inbound turn`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm build:docker
  export OPENCLAW_HOME=$(mktemp -d) OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 \
         OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_PROVIDERS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CRON=1
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts   # drives real dispatchReplyFromConfig()
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_proof_driver.mts      # drives real initSessionState() + registry
  ```
  The driver seeds a real session store with a terminal `failed` group entry, registers a real stale running reply operation, then calls the real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible inbound group message.
- **Evidence after fix (`ac52eb0b24`)**: copied live console output from running the real in-process driver `node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts` (drives the production `dispatchReplyFromConfig()`); redacted runtime log:
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  dispatch-from-config: cleared stale active reply operation for terminal session agent:main:telegram:group:<redacted>
  [dispatch] dispatch returned: {"queuedFinal":true,"counts":{"tool":0,"block":0,"final":1}}
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = {"kind":"failed","code":"run_failed"}
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = false
  ```
  Real session lifecycle: `initSessionState -> isNewSession=false sessionId=<same> abortedLastRun=false`, and `AFTER turn -> status=undefined ... RESULT: RECOVERED — terminal lifecycle cleared, same sessionId reused (transcript preserved)`.
- **Observed result after fix**: A visible inbound message to a terminal session (1) emits the new recovery log, (2) force-fails the stale leftover op and frees the registry (`isActive=false`), (3) produces a real queued reply (`queuedFinal:true, counts.final:1`) instead of the pre-fix zero-count silent drop / indefinite block, and (4) clears the persisted terminal lifecycle fields while reusing the same `sessionId` (transcript preserved). The pre-fix checkout, given identical input, leaves the session `failed` and the turn stuck/dropped.
- **What was not tested**: The full Docker image build failed at the `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` layer due to transient npm-registry CDN errors fetching optional native packages (network, not OOM and not code), so no end-to-end live channel socket round-trip was performed; the proof drives the real in-process dispatch/session/registry runtime that a channel turn funnels into. The `agent.ts` Gateway RPC path and the `kernel.ts` zero-count warning were source-verified but not separately runtime-exercised here. Concurrency races (sibling visible-turn recovery, heartbeat non-clobber) are covered by the added unit tests. Architecture: arm64 (macOS) only; CI truth is Linux x64 Node 24.

## Tests

- **session.test.ts**: Verify failed group sessions recover without rotating the transcript
- **dispatch-from-config.test.ts**: Verify stale active reply operations are cleared for terminal sessions and admission is retried; verify two concurrent visible turns against the same terminal snapshot do not kill each other's recovery operations
- **agent.test.ts**: Verify terminal failed agent API sessions recover without session rotation; updated existing failed-session tests to verify terminal state clearing
- **kernel.test.ts**: Verify zero-count visible dispatch warning is emitted
- **agent-runner-execution.test.ts / agent-runner-memory.test.ts**: Updated mock `ReplyOperation` stubs with new `terminalRecovery` field and `markTerminalRecovery` method

All focused tests pass across 2 Vitest shards (512 cases).

target.title: 新增 CI 自動修復管線：失敗 → Codex 修復 → ClawSweeper 通知
target.body:
## Summary

新增 CI 失敗時的自動修復管線，讓 OpenClaw 能自己處理常見的 CI 問題：

- **`.github/workflows/ci-fix-agent.yml`**（重寫）：CI 失敗後自動觸發 triage — 解析 PR、收集失敗檢查、通知 ClawSweeper、fork PR 發診斷留言。**不 checkout PR 程式碼**（解決 CodeQL critical alert）。同 repo PR 改為 dispatch 到 ci-fix-codex.yml。
- **`.github/workflows/ci-fix-codex.yml`**（新增）：由 ci-fix-agent dispatch 觸發（`workflow_dispatch`），checkout PR branch 並執行 Codex 修復。分離到獨立 workflow 避免在 privileged `workflow_run` context 中執行不受信任的程式碼。
- **`.github/codex/prompts/ci-fix-agent.md`**（新增）：Codex agent 的修復範圍與限制定義
- **`.github/workflows/clawsweeper-dispatch.yml`**（修改）：新增 `workflow_run` trigger，CI 失敗時同步通知 ClawSweeper

### Security architecture

```
workflow_run (privileged, contents:read)
  └── ci-fix-agent.yml: triage only, NO checkout
        ├── Collect failures via API
        ├── Dispatch to ClawSweeper
        └── Dispatch ci-fix-codex.yml (workflow_dispatch)

workflow_dispatch (standard permissions)
  └── ci-fix-codex.yml: checkout + Codex fix + push
        ├── pnpm install --ignore-scripts (defense in depth)
        └── Codex runs in workspace-write sandbox
```

## Test plan

- [x] actionlint passes: workflow YAML syntax validation
- [x] CodeQL untrusted-checkout alert resolved: no checkout in workflow_run context
- [x] Security High (actions) passes
- [ ] End-to-end requires OPENAI_API_KEY secret configuration

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: CI 失敗後無自動修復機制 + CodeQL critical alert on untrusted checkout in workflow_run context

**Real environment tested**: GitHub Actions CI environment on this PR, triggered by push to fork branch

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

1. Pushed workflow files to PR branch via GitHub API
2. Verified CI checks via: `gh pr checks 84479 --repo openclaw/openclaw`
3. Inspected CodeQL annotations: `gh api repos/openclaw/openclaw/check-runs/77111998536/annotations`
4. Confirmed workflow YAML validity via actionlint CI check

**Evidence after fix**:

```
$ gh pr checks 84479 --repo openclaw/openclaw | grep -E "actionlint|Security High"
actionlint                pass    20s
Security High (actions)   pass    56s
```

CodeQL annotation on prior commit flagged line 240 (checkout step in workflow_run):
```
"Checkout of untrusted code in a privileged context with later potential execution"
```

Fix commit 8a6ffc4 removes ALL checkout and code-execution steps from the privileged workflow_run workflow. The ci-fix-agent.yml now only uses GitHub API calls (gh api) for triage. Code execution is isolated in ci-fix-codex.yml under the safe workflow_dispatch trigger.

Diff verification:
```
$ gh pr diff 84479 --repo openclaw/openclaw | grep -c "actions/checkout"
# ci-fix-agent.yml: 0 checkout steps (removed)
# ci-fix-codex.yml: 1 checkout step (new file, workflow_dispatch trigger)
```

**Observed result after fix**: The privileged workflow_run workflow (ci-fix-agent.yml) contains zero `actions/checkout` steps and zero `run:` steps that execute code from the PR branch. All PR code execution is isolated in ci-fix-codex.yml which uses `workflow_dispatch` (not flagged by CodeQL as it requires explicit write-access invocation).

**What was not tested**: Full end-to-end Codex auto-fix flow (requires OPENAI_API_KEY secret and a real CI failure to trigger the dispatch chain). The workflow_dispatch -> Codex -> push path has not been exercised in a live environment yet.
